text: This video is intended for patients hoping to learn more about their upcoming surgery. Viewer discretion is advised.

The video shows a large liver cyst compressing the kidney, occupying much of the upper abdominal space. The tumor is visible in the upper aspect of the kidney near the hilum, where the renal arteries and veins are located. Two renal arteries and two main renal veins are observed, originating from the aorta and connecting to the kidney.

The surgeon removes the liver cyst to create working space in the abdomen, then places a retractor under the liver to elevate it and access the kidney beneath. The kidney is surrounded by normal fat and connective tissue, which is incised to expose the fascia overlying the kidney.

The surgeon uses the fourth arm of the robotic system to assist with retraction. After removing the initial layers of fascia, the inferior vena cava is clearly visible, along with the patient's two renal veins. The gonadal vein is identified as it drains into the vena cava and is dissected downward to expose the ureter located behind it.

The surgeon observes the ureter as it intermittently contracts, then elevates it to expose the plane beneath the kidney and above the psoas muscle. Superficial veins are cauterized and divided to reveal the lower renal artery, which is identified and dissected for clamping during tumor resection.

A vessel loop is placed around the renal artery for gentle retraction. The surgeon uses a stapler to ligate the lower renal vein, which overlies the main renal artery, while ensuring the upper vein remains intact. The main renal artery is dissected behind the upper renal vein, preparing it for clamping during tumor resection. The renal hilum is dissected, and the connective tissue over the kidney is opened, removing fat to expose the tumor.

The surgeon retracts the overlying fat upwards to expose the kidney capsule beneath. The junction of the tumor with the normal kidney is visible, along with its proximity to the renal hilum. The planned margin of resection around the tumor is marked with cautery on the kidney capsule. An ultrasound with picture-in-picture capability is used to verify that the resection margin is deep enough to avoid cutting into the tumor. The tumor is clearly visible and prepared for resection.

The surgeon pre-places sutures to close the defect post-tumor removal and positions clamps on the renal arteries. The tumor removal begins with clamping the arteries to prevent bleeding, aiming to minimize the duration of blood flow interruption to preserve kidney function.

The surgeon injects a fluorescent dye, ICG, and uses a laser light to visualize areas with blood flow, which appear green. The kidney and tumor do not light up, confirming successful clamping of the renal artery. The surgeon begins resecting the tumor, taking a small margin of normal kidney tissue to ensure complete removal.

The surgeon completes the excision of the tumor and sets it aside for later retrieval. The defect in the kidney is repaired by suturing the small veins and arteries at the base, followed by closing the hole with sutures and applying clips to prevent bleeding. After removing the clamps, blood flow is restored to the kidney without any bleeding observed. A second layer of clips is applied to each suture for added security.

The excised tumor is placed into a retrieval bag. Hemostatic agents are applied over the surgical field to reduce venous oozing. A drain is positioned over the kidney to manage potential bleeding or urine leakage, intended for removal before patient discharge. The procedure concludes with the extraction of the tumor and closure of the incision sites.
